required.title: [Read Aloud] Rich, varied picture books and chapter books read daily
[For Mentor] A good dictionary (physical, not just digital)

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Any book by Patricia Polacco, Cynthia Rylant, or Kevin Henkes
suggested.author: Patricia Polacco, Cynthia Rylant, or Kevin Henkes

suggested.title: [Explore Together] Nature walks with naming practice

competence_markers.description: The child consistently notices unfamiliar words during read-alouds and asks about their meaning, or attempts to figure them out from context
The child can name common objects, animals, and plants with increasing specificity — not just generic categories but specific types
The child can provide at least one synonym and one antonym for common words (big/small, happy/sad, fast/slow, and others)
The child can use context clues to make reasonable guesses about unfamiliar word meanings
The child can produce the Big Five (hello, please, thank you, yes, no) in at least one language other than English with acceptable pronunciation
The child can count to ten in their target language
The child maintains a word journal and can discuss words they have collected — their meanings and why they are interesting
The child demonstrates growing precision in their own speech — choosing more specific words rather than defaulting to vague ones

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a new word you learned this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What does it mean? Where did you hear it? Can you use it in a sentence?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe something you see right now using the most specific, precise words you can.
narration_prompts.detail: Do not say "big" — find a better word. Do not say "nice" — find a word that tells me exactly what is nice about it.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me three words that mean almost the same thing as "happy."
narration_prompts.detail: How are they different from each other? When would you use each one?

narration_prompts.prompt: You heard a word you did not know in a story. How did you figure out what it meant?
narration_prompts.detail: What clues did the other words give you?

narration_prompts.prompt: Say hello, please, and thank you in the language you are learning.
narration_prompts.detail: Why do these words matter? What happens when you use them with someone who speaks that language?
Why is it better to know many words than only a few?

cross_connections.description: The structural awareness built in 3.1.1 — that words have jobs (nouns name, verbs describe actions, adjectives describe qualities) — provides the scaffolding for vocabulary work. The child who knows that "enormous" is an adjective understands where it fits in a sentence and how to use it correctly. 3.1.3-A1 fills the structure with content; 3.1.1-A provides the architecture.

When the child learns survival words in another language, they may encounter those words in a different writing system. The awareness from 3.1.2 that other scripts exist — and are learnable — supports the survival vocabulary work. The child who writes their Big Five words in both Latin script and the target language's script is connecting these sub-domains naturally.

The narrations and word journal entries in this packet build writing skills directly. The child who records a new word with a definition and a sentence is practicing the same composition skills developed in 3.2.1. Vocabulary and writing reinforce each other at every level.

The survival vocabulary concept — words you need to stay safe and meet basic needs — connects directly to the physical survival skills in 2.4.1. The child who is learning to identify edible plants and find clean water is also learning to name those things precisely. "Water" is survival vocabulary in every sense.

The stories of early civilizations are rich with unfamiliar vocabulary — "ziggurat," "pharaoh," "cuneiform," "irrigation," "civilization" itself. History reading is one of the richest vocabulary-building activities, and the naming precision developed here makes historical narration more detailed and vivid.

Scientific observation requires precise naming — not "rock" but "granite" or "limestone"; not "bug" but "beetle" or "caterpillar." The naming skills built in this packet directly serve the observational precision that science requires.

content.why_this_matters:
Words are tools. Every word you learn is a new tool you can use to say exactly what you mean, to ask for exactly what you need, and to understand exactly what someone else is telling you. A person who knows many words and uses them carefully is a person who can think more clearly, ask better questions, and solve more problems than a person who does not.

Right now, you already know hundreds of words. You use them every day without thinking about it. But here is something most people never notice: **the words you know shape what you can think about.** If you do not have a word for something, it is harder to think about that thing. If you have the exact right word, the thought becomes clear and sharp.

This packet is about waking up to words. You are going to start noticing the words around you — listening for new ones, wondering what they mean, discovering that many words are connected to each other in surprising ways. You are going to learn that every language on earth has words for the things people need most — food, water, help, thank you, danger — and that learning even a few of these words in another language gives you a kind of power: the power to communicate with people who do not speak your language.

You are also going to learn something important about being precise. "Big" is a word. But "enormous" is a better word when you mean really, truly big. "Gigantic" is another option. "Tiny" means small, but "microscopic" means so small you need a special tool to see it. The person who says "that building is tall" and the person who says "that building is towering" are both correct — but the second person painted a better picture in your mind.

Words matter. The more words you know, and the more carefully you choose them, the more powerful your thinking and your communication become. That is what this packet develops.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Word curiosity** — the habit of noticing unfamiliar words and wanting to know what they mean, rather than skipping over them
2. **Naming precision** — the ability to name things specifically rather than vaguely; not just "bird" but "robin" or "hawk"; not just "tree" but "oak" or "maple"
3. **Simple synonyms and antonyms** — the understanding that many words mean similar things (big/large/huge) and that many words mean opposite things (hot/cold, fast/slow, brave/afraid)
4. **Context clues** — the beginning skill of figuring out what an unfamiliar word probably means from the words around it
5. **First survival vocabulary in another language** — hello, please, thank you, yes, no, help — in at least one language other than English
6. **The connection between words and thinking** — the foundational insight that knowing more words means being able to think more thoughts
content.core_content:
### Part 1: You Already Know More Words Than You Think

How many words do you know? If you are around five years old, you probably know somewhere between 2,000 and 5,000 words. That sounds like a lot — and it is. But an adult who reads regularly knows between 20,000 and 35,000 words. And the English language has over 170,000 words in current use.

That means there are thousands and thousands of words out there that you have not met yet. Every one of them means something. Every one of them describes a piece of the world that you could know about but do not — yet.

Here is the good news: you have been learning words since before you could walk. You learned them by hearing them — over and over, in different situations, until they stuck. That is still the best way to learn words. Listen carefully. Notice words you do not know. Ask what they mean. Use them yourself. That is the whole method.

### Part 2: The Power of the Right Word

Imagine you are trying to tell someone about a dog you saw. You could say: "I saw a big dog."

That works. But what if the dog was not just big — what if it was the biggest dog you had ever seen? You could say:

- "I saw an **enormous** dog."
- "I saw a **massive** dog."
- "I saw a **gigantic** dog."

Each of these words means "very big" — but each one paints a slightly different picture. "Enormous" sounds serious. "Gigantic" sounds almost unbelievable. "Massive" makes you think of weight and strength.

Now imagine the dog was small. You could say "I saw a small dog." Or you could say:

- "I saw a **tiny** dog."
- "I saw a **miniature** dog."
- "I saw a **teacup-sized** dog."

The more words you know, the more exactly you can describe what you see, what you feel, what you think, and what you need. A person with ten words for "big" can describe the world more precisely than a person with one.

### Part 3: Words Have Families

Many words are related to each other — like members of a family.

**Words that mean similar things are called synonyms.** Happy, glad, joyful, cheerful, delighted — these are all synonyms. They all point toward the same feeling, but each one has its own flavor. "Happy" is the most common. "Delighted" is stronger — it means you are not just happy but pleasantly surprised by your happiness. "Cheerful" suggests you are showing your happiness on the outside.

**Words that mean opposite things are called antonyms.** Hot and cold. Fast and slow. Light and dark. Brave and afraid. Generous and stingy. Antonyms help you understand words by showing you what they are not. If you know what "brave" means, then you also understand "cowardly" — it means the opposite.

**Words that sound alike but mean different things are called homophones.** "Bear" (the animal) and "bare" (without covering). "Night" (when it is dark) and "knight" (a warrior in armor). "Flower" (a plant) and "flour" (what you bake with). English is full of these, and they are both tricky and fascinating.

Learning to notice these family connections helps you learn words faster. When you learn one new word, you can often connect it to words you already know — and suddenly you have learned two or three words instead of one.

### Part 4: Context Clues — Detective Work with Words

What happens when you hear a word you do not know? You could stop and ask someone — and you should do that often. But sometimes you can figure it out from the words around it. This is called using **context clues**.

Listen to this sentence: "The sky was overcast, so we brought our umbrellas."

If you do not know what "overcast" means, the rest of the sentence gives you a clue. You brought umbrellas — which means it might rain — which means the sky is probably cloudy and gray. That is exactly what "overcast" means: covered with clouds.

Here is another one: "The kitten was timid. It hid behind the couch whenever someone new came into the room."

What does "timid" mean? The kitten hides when strangers appear. It must be shy or scared of new things. That is what "timid" means: easily frightened, shy.

You will not always be able to figure out a new word from context. Sometimes the clues are not enough. But the habit of looking for clues — of treating an unfamiliar word like a puzzle to solve — is one of the most important thinking habits you can develop. Good readers do this automatically. You are going to practice it until you do too.

### Part 5: Naming the World Precisely

Look around the room you are in right now. How many things can you name?

Most people would say "table, chair, lamp, book, wall, floor, window." That is fine. But could you be more specific?

- Not just "table" but **dining table** or **desk** or **coffee table** or **workbench**
- Not just "chair" but **armchair** or **stool** or **rocking chair** or **bench**
- Not just "lamp" but **floor lamp** or **desk lamp** or **chandelier** or **lantern**

The same principle applies outside. Not just "tree" but **oak, maple, pine, birch, willow**. Not just "bird" but **robin, sparrow, crow, hawk, hummingbird**. Not just "flower" but **daisy, sunflower, tulip, rose, violet**.

Why does this matter? Because the person who can name things precisely can think about them precisely. The child who knows the difference between an oak and a maple actually sees the world differently than the child who sees only "trees." They notice the shape of the leaves. They notice where different trees grow. They notice the acorns under the oak and the helicopter seeds spinning down from the maple.

Precise naming is not showing off. It is seeing clearly.

### Part 6: Words in Other Languages — Your First Survival Vocabulary

Every language on earth has words for the things people need most. Food. Water. Help. Thank you. Danger. Yes. No.

You are going to learn a small set of the most important words in at least one language other than English. These are called **survival words** — the words you would need if you traveled to a place where people spoke a different language.

**The Big Five** are the most powerful words you can learn in any language:
1. **Hello** — because every conversation starts here
2. **Please** — because asking politely opens doors that demanding cannot
3. **Thank you** — because gratitude is respected everywhere on earth
4. **Yes** — because agreement must be clear
5. **No** — because refusal must also be clear

When you learn the Big Five in another language and use them when you meet someone who speaks that language, something remarkable happens. The other person sees that you tried. You did not expect them to speak your language — you made the effort to speak theirs. Even if you only know five words, that effort earns respect.

Beyond the Big Five, you will learn a few more survival words:
- **Water** and **food** — basic needs
- **Help** — the most important emergency word in any language
- **I don't understand** — which, strangely, is one of the most useful phrases you can learn, because it tells the other person to slow down or try a different way
- Numbers **one through ten** — because numbers unlock buying, counting, and understanding time

You do not need to speak another language perfectly. You need to be willing to try. That willingness is the beginning of everything.
content.formative_practices:
*Vocabulary at Tier A1 is built through reading, conversation, observation, and play — not through drills or memorization exercises. The following studies guide the mentor and student through the packet's content over several weeks.*

### Study 1: The Daily Read-Aloud with Word Noticing [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read aloud daily from high-quality picture books and early chapter books. After each reading, ask: "Did you hear any words that were new to you? Any words that sounded interesting?" When the child identifies a word, pause, discuss it, and add it to the word journal. If the child does not identify any, the mentor picks one rich word from the reading and introduces it: "Did you notice the word 'magnificent'? That means really, really wonderful and impressive. What would you call magnificent?"

### Study 2: The Naming Walk [Early in the Packet, Repeated]
Take a walk — outside, through a store, around the house — and name everything as precisely as possible. Challenge each other: "That is not just a bird — what kind of bird?" "That is not just a tree — can we figure out what kind?" Bring a field guide or use a reference book afterward to identify things you could not name. The goal is precision and curiosity, not perfection.

### Study 3: Synonym and Antonym Games [Mid-Packet]
Play games with word families. The mentor says a word; the child gives a synonym. Then the mentor says a word; the child gives an antonym. Start with easy pairs (big/small, happy/sad, hot/cold) and build toward more interesting ones (brave/timid, generous/selfish, ancient/modern). Make it playful — this is a game, not a test.

### Study 4: The Big Five in Another Language [Mid-Packet]
Choose a target language — ideally one connected to the family's heritage, community, or travel plans, or the language being studied in 3.1.1. Learn hello, please, thank you, yes, and no with correct pronunciation. Practice them daily. If possible, use them with a native speaker — at a restaurant, with a community member, or via a video recording. Add counting to ten once the Big Five are solid.

### Study 5: Context Clue Practice [Mid-to-Late Packet]
During read-alouds, when an unfamiliar word appears, pause before explaining it: "What do you think that word might mean? What clues do the other words give you?" Let the child guess. Their guess does not need to be perfect — the habit of guessing before being told is the skill. After guessing, confirm or correct and discuss.

### Study 6: The Word Collection [Ongoing, Culminating Late Packet]
The child maintains a personal word journal throughout the packet. At the end, review the collection together. How many words did you collect? Which is your favorite? Which was the hardest? Can you use three of your new words in a sentence? The journal is a physical record of growth — the child can see how many new tools they have gathered.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Word Curiosity
**Developing well:** The child spontaneously asks about unfamiliar words. During read-alouds, they interrupt with "What does that mean?" They notice interesting words on signs, in conversations, and in daily life. They enjoy adding words to their journal.

**Developing slowly:** The child never asks about unfamiliar words. They glaze over unknown words without noticing. The word journal feels like a chore.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you reading books with rich enough vocabulary? If the books are too simple, there is nothing new to notice. Are you modeling curiosity yourself? If you never comment on words, the child has no model for the behavior.

### 2. Naming Precision
**Developing well:** The child is starting to use specific names instead of generic ones. They say "robin" instead of "bird" (at least sometimes). They notice differences between things and want to know the right name.

**Developing slowly:** The child uses only generic category words. Everything is "stuff," "thing," "nice," or "big."

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done the naming walks? Have you provided the child with access to field guides, picture dictionaries, and labeled images? Precision requires exposure to precise vocabulary — the child cannot use words they have never heard.

### 3. Synonym and Antonym Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can play synonym and antonym games with engagement. They understand that multiple words can mean similar things and can suggest alternatives. They may spontaneously note: "That is like when you say..."

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot think of any words that mean the same thing as common words. The concept of opposites is shaky.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you made this playful? Games, not drills. Start with the easiest, most physical pairs (hot/cold, up/down) and build gradually. If you jumped to abstract pairs too quickly, back up.

### 4. Context Clue Use
**Developing well:** When prompted, the child makes reasonable guesses about unfamiliar words based on surrounding context. Their guesses are in the right ballpark even when not perfectly accurate.

**Developing slowly:** The child says "I don't know" immediately without attempting to use context. They do not look at surrounding words for clues.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you giving the child time and encouragement to guess before you supply the answer? The habit of guessing is the skill — not the accuracy of the guess. If you always define words immediately, the child has no reason to develop this skill.

### 5. Survival Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The child can produce the Big Five in their target language without prompting. Pronunciation is recognizable. They enjoy using these words and look for opportunities to do so.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot remember the Big Five even with prompting. Pronunciation is unclear.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you practicing daily? Short, consistent practice — thirty seconds of "how do you say hello in [language]?" — works better than weekly drilling sessions. Is the child hearing native pronunciation (from recordings, native speakers, or high-quality audio)?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Turning Vocabulary into a Test
If you quiz the child on definitions, you will kill the curiosity this packet is trying to build. "What does 'enormous' mean?" asked as a test question produces anxiety. "Remember that word 'enormous' from the story? I just saw an enormous truck outside!" produces delight. Use new words in conversation. Point them out in the world. Let the child encounter them repeatedly in natural contexts. That is how vocabulary becomes real.

### Choosing Books That Are Too Easy
If the child already knows every word in the book, there is no vocabulary growth happening. Books should be enjoyable AND slightly challenging. The sweet spot is books where the child understands the story easily but encounters two or three words per reading that are new or only partially known.

### Ignoring Pronunciation in the Target Language
When the child learns survival words in another language, pronunciation matters more than spelling. A child who says "gracias" with confident, roughly-correct pronunciation will be understood. A child who has memorized the spelling but says it in a way no native speaker would recognize has learned nothing functional. Use audio resources. If possible, have the child practice with native speakers.

### Rushing Past the Foundation
This packet is about building habits — word curiosity, naming precision, the practice of noticing. These habits take time. Do not rush to more advanced vocabulary work before these habits are established. A child who finishes this packet with fifty words in their journal and a genuine love of discovering new ones is better prepared for A2 than a child who can define two hundred words but finds the whole enterprise tedious.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: A New Word
**Strong:** The child names a specific word, defines it in their own language, remembers where they encountered it, and uses it in a sentence. They show enthusiasm about the word.
**Weak:** "I don't remember any new words." Or names a word but cannot explain what it means.

### Prompt 2: Precise Description
**Strong:** The child uses specific, descriptive language — colors, textures, sizes, shapes, comparisons. Instead of "a nice flower" they say "a tall sunflower with big yellow petals."
**Weak:** Everything is described with "big," "nice," "cool," or "stuff."

### Prompt 3: Synonyms
**Strong:** The child names at least two synonyms and can explain how they differ: "Joyful is like you are really, really happy. Cheerful is like you are showing it."
**Weak:** Cannot name any synonyms or names them but cannot distinguish between them.

### Prompt 4: Context Clues
**Strong:** The child describes the process: "The sentence said they brought umbrellas, so I thought maybe the word meant cloudy or rainy."
**Weak:** "I just guessed." Or cannot describe any process for figuring out unfamiliar words.

### Prompt 5: Survival Vocabulary
**Strong:** Produces the words with confidence and acceptable pronunciation. Can explain why they matter: "It is polite" or "They see you are trying."
**Weak:** Cannot produce the words or produces them with pronunciation that would not be understood.

### Prompt 6: Why Words Matter
**Strong:** Articulates the connection between words and thinking or communication, even simply: "If you know more words you can say exactly what you mean" or "You can talk to more people."
**Weak:** "I don't know" or "Because my mom told me to learn them."
